title: Compiled AI: Deterministic Code Generation for LLM-Based Workflow Automation
summary_desc: This research introduces “compiled AI,” a system where large language models generate deterministic code for workflow automation, prioritizing reliability and auditability in high-stakes environments like healthcare. The approach utilizes constrained LLM code generation within validated templates to achieve predictable execution and reduce operational costs. A four-stage pipeline converts probabilistic outputs into production code, measured through metrics like token amortization and security. Evaluations on function calling (BFCL) and document intelligence (DocILE) demonstrate significant efficiency gains – including 96% task completion with zero tokens in BFCL and 80% key field extraction accuracy in DocILE – alongside robust security performance.
authors: Geert Trooskens; Aaron Karlsberg; Anmol Sharma; Lamara De Brouwer; Max Van Puyvelde; Matthew Young; John Thickstun; Gil Alterovitz; Walter A. De Brouwer

title: Blueprint First, Model Second: A Framework for Deterministic LLM Workflow
summary_desc: This research introduces the \textsc{Source Code Agent} framework, a novel approach to utilizing large language models (LLMs) in deterministic workflows by separating high-level planning from low-level execution. The “Blueprint First, Model Second” philosophy involves translating operational procedures into executable source code blueprints, executed by a deterministic engine while reserving the LLM for specific subtasks. Evaluations on the TravelPlanner benchmark demonstrate a significant improvement – a 35.56% final pass rate – alongside substantial reductions in constraint violations and increased execution efficiency compared to existing methods. Furthermore, the framework’s adaptability was validated through deployments and results across diverse procedural workflows like incident diagnosis.
authors: Libin Qiu; Yuhang Ye; Zhirong Gao; Xide Zou; Junfu Chen; Ziming Gui; Weizhi Huang; Xiaobo Xue; Wenkai Qiu; Kun Zhao

title: LLM+P: Empowering Large Language Models with Optimal Planning Proficiency
summary_desc: This research introduces LLM+P, a novel framework that combines large language models with classical planners to address the limitations of LLMs in long-horizon planning. The system translates natural language problem descriptions into PDDL files, utilizes planners for efficient solution finding, and then converts the plan back into natural language output. Experiments on diverse benchmark problems demonstrate that LLM+P consistently produces optimal plans, significantly outperforming standard LLMs which typically fail to generate even feasible solutions.  The findings highlight a promising approach for enhancing LLM capabilities in complex planning tasks.
authors: Bo Liu; Yuqian Jiang; Xiaohan Zhang; Qiang Liu; Shiqi Zhang; Joydeep Biswas; Peter Stone

title: MCP-Solver: Integrating Language Models with Constraint Programming Systems
summary_desc: The MCP Solver introduces an innovative approach by combining Large Language Models with traditional constraint programming systems via the Model Context Protocol. This system overcomes a key limitation of LLMs – their lack of formal reasoning – by granting them access to solvers like Minizinc, PySAT, and Z3. The core method involves an iterative validation editing process to maintain model consistency during modifications and facilitate structured problem refinement. Ultimately, MCP-Solver aims to enhance LLM capabilities through the integration of symbolic solving techniques.

title: DUPLEX: Agentic Dual-System Planning via LLM-Driven Information Extraction
summary_desc: DUPLEX is a novel neuro-symbolic architecture designed to improve the reliability of LLM-based task planning by separating information extraction from logical reasoning. It employs a “Fast System” that uses an LLM for deterministic PDDL problem creation and a “Slow System” which iteratively refines plans using an LLM when the initial plan fails, guided by solver diagnostics. Evaluations across diverse planning domains show DUPLEX achieves superior success rates and reliability compared to existing methods. The core contribution lies in restricting the LLM’s role to structured semantic grounding while relying on a symbolic planner for logical planning synthesis.
authors: Keru Hua; Ding Wang; Yaoying Gu; Xiaoguang Ma

title: Beyond Monolithic Models: Symbolic Seams for Composable Neuro-Symbolic Architectures
summary_desc: This research proposes a new approach to designing AI systems by advocating for composability—treating them as evolving architectures rather than static models. The authors introduce “symbolic seams,” explicit architectural boundaries that incorporate inspectable components and versioned constraints, allowing for the combination of neural networks and symbolic reasoning. These seams enable a neuro-symbolic architecture capable of leveraging both the adaptability of learned models and the verifiability of explicit rules. Ultimately, the paper argues this design fosters intelligent systems that are more extensible, transparent, and easier to evolve through principled modification.
authors: Nicolas Schuler; Vincenzo Scotti; Raffaela Mirandola
